Todoist vs Arc Browser: Which Is Better?

Choosing between Todoist and Arc Browser? Here's a side-by-side comparison to help you decide.

Quick verdict: Choose Todoist if you need freelancers or solo-devs. Choose Arc Browser if you prioritize developers or designers.

Todoist

PricingFree / $4/mo Pro / $6/mo Business
Rating★★★★ 4.4/5

Key Features

  • Natural language parsing for instant task creation
  • Labels, filters, and custom views for flexible task organization
  • Cross-platform apps (Mac, Windows, iOS, Android, web, wearables)
  • Project templates and shared projects for recurring workflows
  • Productivity tracker and goal-setting with progress visualization
  • Integrations with Zapier, IFTTT, Google Calendar, and 60+ other tools

Pros

  • Natural language input is the gold standard — 'pay rent on the 1st every month at 9am' parses perfectly every time
  • Fast doesn't begin to describe it; the app opens instantly on every platform and sync is near-instant
  • Labels, filters, and custom views let you slice your tasks however you want (by energy level, context, project)
  • Karma gamification is subtle and actually motivating without being annoying
  • The collaboration features are simple and effective for shared shopping lists, small team tasks, or family chores

Cons

  • Not built for complex project management — no Gantt charts, no dependencies, no resource management
  • Collaboration is basic; you can share projects but don't expect granular permissions or review workflows
  • The free tier is good but limits you to 5 active projects and some features are gated behind Pro
  • No real file management — you can attach files, but there's no organized file storage per project
  • Boards/kanban view was added late and feels bolted on compared to native kanban tools
Try Todoist →

Arc Browser

PricingFree
Rating★★★★ 4.4/5

Key Features

  • Vertical sidebar tabs with visual tab previews
  • Spaces for separating browsing contexts (work, personal, project, etc.)
  • Split View for side-by-side page browsing
  • Easels for visual bookmarking, mood boarding, and idea organization
  • Boosts for customizing website appearance with CSS
  • Command palette (Cmd+T) for tab search, navigation, and actions

Pros

  • Vertical tabs are a genuinely better UI for tab management; you can read tab titles without squinting
  • Spaces let you separate work, personal, and project browsing with their own pinned tabs and profiles
  • Split view for side-by-side pages is built in and smooth to use
  • Easels for visual bookmarking and organizing ideas are a creative alternative to bookmarks
  • Boosts let you customize any website's appearance with CSS modifications (removing distractions, changing colors)

Cons

  • Mac and Windows only at this point — no mobile app beyond iPhone, no Linux support
  • Uses Chromium under the hood, so it inherits Chrome's resource usage and privacy concerns
  • Some features feel beta-quality; occasional crashes and performance hiccups happen
  • The different paradigm (no traditional tabs) can be disorienting for the first week
  • Auto-archiving tabs (Arc's feature that hides unused tabs) can lose track of tabs you meant to keep

Which Should You Choose?

Both Todoist and Arc Browser are excellent tools in their category. The right choice depends on your specific needs:

  • Choose Todoist if: Todoist is the rare productivity tool that's both powerful enough for GTD nerds and simple enough for people who just want to stop forgetting things. The magic is the natural language input — type 'buy groceries every Saturday at 10am' and it just works. It's been around for 15 years and somehow still feels modern.
  • Choose Arc Browser if: Arc is the browser that reimagines what a browser can be. Instead of a horizontal tab bar that turns into a mess of tiny icons, Arc puts tabs vertically in a sidebar, organizes them into Spaces and Profiles, and adds a command palette for navigation. It's a controversial take — some people love it, some find it unnecessary — but it's the most innovative browser since the original Chrome.
We may earn a commission if you purchase through links. All opinions are our own.
